elements.sent-2411-001: Extended Reality (XR) services are set to transform applications over $5^{th$ and $6^{th}$ generation wireless networks, delivering immersive experiences.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Concurrently, Artificial Intelligence (AI) advancements have expanded their role in wireless networks, however, trust and transparency in AI remain to be strengthened.
elements.sent-2411-003: Thus, providing explanations for AI-enabled systems can enhance trust.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce Value Function Factorization (VFF)-based Explainable (X) Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) algorithms, explaining reward design in XR codec adaptation through reward decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-005: We contribute four enhancements to XMARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-006: Firstly, we detail architectural modifications to enable reward decomposition in VFF-based MARL algorithms: Value Decomposition Networks (VDN), Mixture of Q-Values (QMIX), and Q-Transformation (Q-TRAN).
elements.sent-2411-007: Secondly, inspired by multi-task learning, we reduce the overhead of vanilla XMARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-008: Thirdly, we propose a new explainability metric, Reward Difference Fluctuation Explanation (RDFX), suitable for problems with adjustable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-009: Lastly, we propose adaptive XMARL, leveraging network gradients and reward decomposition for improved action selection.
elements.sent-2411-010: Simulation results indicate that, in XR codec adaptation, the Packet Delivery Ratio reward is the primary contributor to optimal performance compared to the initial composite reward, which included delay and Data Rate Ratio components.
elements.sent-2411-011: Modifications to VFF-based XMARL algorithms, incorporating multi-headed structures and adaptive loss functions, enable the best-performing algorithm, Multi-Headed Adaptive (MHA)-QMIX, to achieve significant average gains over the Adjust Packet Size baseline up to $10.7\%$, $41.4\%$, $33.3\%$, and $67.9\%$ in XR index, jitter, delay, and Packet Loss Ratio (PLR), respectively.
elements.sent-2411-012: Applications of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) across many industries have been on the rise recently and the new generations of wireless networks $5^{th}$ (5G) and $6^{th}$ (6G) have not been exceptions.
elements.sent-2411-013: AI presents great potential in optimizing numerous functionalities in wireless networks and can provide autonomy in situations where human intervention is not feasible due to high complexity.
elements.sent-2411-014: This capability has led to the integration of AI-based algorithms in many critical applications within the telecommunications industry.
elements.sent-2411-015: These algorithms are often essential components of network architecture, including deep neural networks, which are frequently considered black boxes from a human perspective \cite{adadi2018peeking .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Evidently, this raises concerns regarding trust.
elements.sent-2411-017: Additionally, faulty human design can significantly impact confidence in AI-based systems, especially during the early design stages.
elements.sent-2411-018: The necessity for trust in AI systems has been a recurring topic among researchers, and due to its extensive use, it has had an impact on society.
elements.sent-2411-019: For instance, in June 2022, the Canadian Government presented the AI and Data Act (AIDA) \cite{TheArtif85:online where it is stated that ``the design, development, and use of AI systems must be safe, and must respect the values of Canadians''.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Similar statements have been proposed around the world, leading the research community to seek ways to guarantee trust and transparency in the usage of AI.
elements.sent-2411-021: For this reason, providing explanations or interpretations of current AI-based solutions using Explainable AI (XAI) is called for.
elements.sent-2411-022: To respond to this call several proposals have been made to find explanations for existing algorithms in the subfield of machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-023: Some comprehensive studies in the literature attempt to classify these efforts, with supervised machine learning algorithms being the most commonly studied area at the moment of writing this paper \cite{burkart2021survey .}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, a handful of surveys exist in the emerging subfield of explainable reinforcement learning \cite{vouros2022explainable, hickling2023explainability, milani2024explainable .}
elements.sent-2411-025: A common categorization divides explainable machine learning methods into two categories: intrinsic and post-hoc \cite{hickling2023explainability .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Intrinsic interpretability involves inherently understandable building models, like small decision trees.
elements.sent-2411-027: In contrast, post-hoc interpretability involves developing a supplementary model to explain an existing one.
elements.sent-2411-028: Despite the valuable information provided by post-hoc methods, all explanations are acquired after obtaining a trained model, making it difficult to gather information online.
elements.sent-2411-029: Additionally, the explainability of Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) algorithms is an even more under-researched area \cite{boggess2023explainable , with only a few papers existing in the literature due to its increased complexity compared to single-agent approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-030: This means that 6G must be equipped with self-sustainability and adapt to the newly offered services bounded to high requirements and user demand.
elements.sent-2411-031: Extended Reality (XR) and Cloud Gaming (CG) are considered among the services that 6G will support.
elements.sent-2411-032: XR has indeed been previously discussed in 3GPP 5G-related documentation \cite{3gpp1, 3gpp2 , however, the leap of 6G in terms of the usage of AI/ML \cite{bang20236g will impact immersive technologies\cite{Fiverese47:online as well.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: In this work, we intend to study XR traffic codec adaptation using Value Function Factorization (VFF)-based Explainable (X) MARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-034: To comply with recent regulations to improve trust in AI-based systems, we delve into Explainable MARL (XMARL) and extend Reward Decomposition \cite{juozapaitis2019explainable in state-of-the-art VFF-based MARL algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Furthermore, network latency, packet loss, and jitter are considered the main network-related Key Performance Indicators for XR services \cite{itug1035 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The implications of the previous metrics in the user experience have been studied consistently.
elements.sent-2411-037: For such a reason in this work, the performance results of the proposed algorithms are shown utilizing the mentioned KPIs.
elements.sent-2411-038: This work differs from the previous works with the following four contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-039: We present a general architecture to enable reward decomposition in MARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-040: We propose alternative solutions to reduce the overhead of vanilla decomposed MARL algorithms using multi-headed structures.
elements.sent-2411-041: We introduce a new metric called Reward Difference Fluctuation Explanations (RDFX) fitted for problems where actions correspond to parameters to optimize.
elements.sent-2411-042: Finally, we present an online/adaptive/explainable solution that uses network gradients and leverage reward decomposition to improve the action selection of the XMARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-043: These contributions enable us to draw conclusions regarding the design of composite rewards in VFF-based MARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-044: In this paper, we focus on explaining XR codec adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-045: However, our findings are agnostic to the use case and can be extended to other applications.
elements.sent-2411-046: By decomposing the objective function, and consequently, the Q-Value functions, we can observe the behavior of each component.
elements.sent-2411-047: This observation led us to propose an adaptive approach that does not require post-hoc analysis to determine the importance of rewards.
elements.sent-2411-048: Specifically, simulation results demonstrate that the best-performing algorithm, Multi-Headed Adaptive (MHA)-QMIX, shows improvement in all studied network Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) over the Adjust Packet Size (APS) algorithm, a baseline introduced in \cite{Bojovic2023, Lagen2023 , by up to $10.7\%$, $41.4\%$, $33.3\%$, and $67.9\%$ in XR index, jitter, delay, and Packet Loss Rate (PLR) respectively.}

elements.subsection-2411-001: Explainable Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Algorithm Taxonomy
elements.sent-2411-077: In this subsection, we introduce a high-level taxonomy of our proposed algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-078: Figure \ref{taxonomy depicts the high-level categories of the taxonomy and their relationship to the RL framework.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The red dashed boxes represent areas of reinforcement learning that are out of the scope of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-080: At the root of the tree, MARL algorithms are split into two main categories: cooperative and non-cooperative.
elements.sent-2411-081: Non-cooperative includes independent and competitive approaches.
elements.sent-2411-082: Meanwhile, cooperative MARL includes VFF methods and non-VFF methods, such as Policy Gradient-based algorithms like MADDPG \cite{lowe2017multi , COMA \cite{foerster2018counterfactual , MAPPO \cite{yu2022surprising , and MATRPO \cite{li2023multiagent , as well as Actor-Critic methods like MASAC \cite{pu2021decomposed and MAAC \cite{iqbal2019actor .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Note that additional algorithms can be classified within these categories, but we have only mentioned the most cited.
elements.sent-2411-084: Finally, at the bottom of the tree, we present our proposed algorithms, inspired by VFF-based MARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-085: Three families are depicted, each corresponding to the main algorithms proposed in this paper: Decomposed Vanilla (D), Decomposed Multi-Headed (MH-D), and Multi-Headed Adaptive (MHA-D) algorithms, respectively.

elements.subsection-2411-002: Markov Decision Model for Cooperative Multi-Agent Tasks
elements.sent-2411-087: Let's define a fully cooperative multi-agent task as a Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (Dec-POMDP) \cite{oliehoek2016concise composed of the tuple $\mathcal{G = <\mathcal{S , \mathcal{A , P, r, \mathcal{Z , O, N, \gamma>$ where $\bm{s \in \mathcal{S}$ corresponds to the state of the environment.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-096: Value Function Factorization (VFF) in Cooperative MARL refers to the decomposition of a joint action-value function into individual value functions or components that can be more easily managed and computed.

elements.sent-2411-098: In the introductory paper\cite{sunehag2017value , the authors introduce the assumption that the joint action-value function could be approximated or factorized in less complex functions as, \begin{equation}\label{vdn_fact} Q^{\pi}_{jt}(s_t, \bm{\tau}_t, \bm{a}_t) = \sum_{n=0}^N Q_n(s_t, \tau_{t,n}, a_{t,n}),\end{equation} where $Q_n(s_t, \tau_{t,i , a_{t,i )$ corresponds to the action-value function of the $n^{th}$ agent.}}}

elements.sent-2411-110: In the next subsection, we introduce Explainable Reinforcement Learning concepts and reward decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-111: Explainable Reinforcement Learning is a recent subfield of Explainable ML that aims to shed light on the interpretation and explanations of RL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-112: Unlike Supervised Learning (SL) and Unsupervised Learning (USL), RL is a sequential decision-making approach where an agent takes actions and seeks to maximize an objective function in the long term using a predefined exploration strategy \cite{sutton2018reinforcement .}
elements.sent-2411-113: Yet, the rationale behind the agent's actions is not always understood \cite{qing2022survey .}
elements.sent-2411-114: In \cite{milani2024explainable a comprehensive taxonomy is described for XRL.}
elements.sent-2411-115: The authors categorize XRL techniques into three groups based on the aspect of the RL agent they aim to explain: feature importance, which seeks explanations for individual actions; learning process and MDP, which finds explanations for influential experiences; and policy-level, which provides insights into long-term behavior.
elements.sent-2411-116: In this work, we explore a technique found in the second group of the previous taxonomy, Decomposed Reward Functions (DRF).
elements.sent-2411-117: The main concept of this technique involves deriving insights from the agent's reward function by decomposing the composite objective function into separate individual objectives \cite{juozapaitis2019explainable .}
elements.sent-2411-118: Formally, a single agent composite reward function can be decomposed in $C$ components as, \begin{equation}R(s_t, a_t) = \sum_{c\in C}R_c(s_t,a_t),\end{equation} where $R_c(s_t, a_t)$ corresponds to an individual objective for state $s_t$ and action $a_t$ in time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-119: Similarly, the authors in \cite{juozapaitis2019explainable claim that $Q^{\pi}$ can be also decomposed as follows, \begin{equation}Q^{\pi}(s_t, a_t) = \sum_{c\in C}Q^{\pi}_c(s_t,a_t),\end{equation} where $Q^{\pi _c(s_t,a_t)$ is the decomposed Q-value function corresponding $R_c$.}}

elements.sent-2411-132: Capturing explanations or insights into the behavior of reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms has been a topic of research within the AI community.
elements.sent-2411-133: In the context of reward decomposition, various metrics have been proposed in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-134: These metrics aim to explain the behavior in terms of action preference for each decomposed reward.
elements.sent-2411-135: This is quite useful due to the direct implications for understanding why one algorithm outperforms another, why a particular action is preferred over others, and which reward is the most significant at the time of action decision.
elements.sent-2411-137: Reward Difference Explanations (RDX): It is defined as $\Delta_c (s, a_x, a_y) = \bm{Q _c(s, a_x) - \bm{Q _c(s,a_y)$ and tries to provide insights about why an agent prefers action $a_x$ over $a_y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: The computed RDX can be plotted to show graphically the action preference per decomposed reward.
elements.sent-2411-139: Positive and Negative MSX (MSX$^+$ and MSX$^-$): This metric aims to provide concise graphical explanations by identifying the minimal number of rewards needed to select an action as RDX dimensions increase with $C$.
elements.sent-2411-140: It introduces the concept of the action disadvantage of $a_x$ over $a_y$ by defining $d:= \sum_{c\in C I[\Delta_c (s, a_x, a_y) < 0] \cdot |\Delta_c (s, a_x, a_y)|$, where $I$ corresponds to the identity function.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Then, two cases are possible: the first comprises the smallest set of positive values $X^+ \in \mathcal{X}$, and the second comprises the smallest set of negative values, $X^- \in \mathcal{X}$, calculated by $d$ for all $a_x$ to outweigh or to fall behind $a_y$, identified as MSX$^+$ and MSX$^-$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-142: MSX$^+$ can formally be written as, \begin{equation}\text{MSX}^+ := \text{argmin}_{X^+ \in 2^C}|X^+| \text{s.t} \sum_{c\in C} \Delta_c (s, a_x, a_y) > d.\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-007: Impact of delay, jitter and loss in QoE for XR services
elements.sent-2411-144: To the best of our knowledge, there are no recommendations or standards that include the impact of general Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) on XR services throughout the literature.
elements.sent-2411-145: However, some efforts have been made.
elements.sent-2411-146: In the recommendation ITU-T G.1035 \cite{itug1035 , three Virtual Reality (VR) QoE influence factors are depicted.}
elements.sent-2411-147: These factors are categorized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-148: Human: This category is related to human subjectivity, such as visual or hearing impairments, immersion capacity, demographics, or emotions-related factors.
elements.sent-2411-149: System: This category is related to the content, video/audio codecs such as bitrate, frame rate, resolution, hardware, and network transmission.
elements.sent-2411-150: Context: This category is related to those aspects that describe a situational influence on the user experience.
elements.sent-2411-151: Network transmission factors are related to the impact of networking indicators such as delay, jitter, or packet loss on VR.
elements.sent-2411-152: In the case of delay, including queuing, over-the-air, and buffering delays, it is one of the main causes of user discomfort due to simulator sickness \cite{9123114 .}
elements.sent-2411-153: Packet loss corresponds to another important factor in the degradation of QoE \cite{8917613 .}
elements.sent-2411-154: High packet losses in reliable transmission protocols increase the number of retransmissions and consequently the network delay.
elements.sent-2411-155: Jitter is described as the variation of the delay time of consecutive packets.
elements.sent-2411-156: The typical reason for large jitter corresponds to network congestion.
elements.sent-2411-157: A large amount of jitter can affect the user perception, especially in voice-related \cite{9642000 , XR \cite{akyildiz2022wireless and haptic \cite{promwongsa2020comprehensive applications.}}}
elements.sent-2411-158: The rest of the factors are not discussed in this work due to being out of scope.
elements.sent-2411-159: Despite 5G having increased considerably the reliability, bandwidth, and reduced latency of its plethora of services, the current XR codec adaptation state-of-the-art algorithms struggle in extreme situations where congestion or environmental losses exist\cite{iturria, Bojovic2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-160: In this paper, we apply explainability to an application, specifically XR codec adaptation over 5G.
elements.sent-2411-161: To this end, we begin by providing the system model as follows.
elements.sent-2411-162: We utilize a 5G network accommodating a group of $N$ XR and CG users receiving downlink traffic from a Base Station (BS).
elements.sent-2411-163: The XR data flow originates from an application server, which periodically receives feedback from the BS at intervals of $t_w$.
elements.sent-2411-164: This feedback loop involves the aggregation of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) from both the BS and the users, facilitating an exchange of information with the application server.
elements.sent-2411-165: With each feedback iteration, the application server dynamically adjusts XR codec parameters to uphold QoE standards.
elements.sent-2411-166: Our experimental setup features three distinct XR users connected to a single BS.
elements.sent-2411-167: To mimic real-world network conditions, we introduce adjustments to certain simulation parameters, such as the capacity of the transmission Radio Link Control buffer (Unacknowledged Mode) and the available bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-168: Additionally, User Equipments (UEs) are spatially distributed across three concentric rings.
elements.sent-2411-169: Notably, the generated XR and CG traffic adheres to the specifications outlined in the 3GPP release-17 study \cite{3gpp3 .}
elements.section-2411-005: XR Codec Adaptation-based Explainable Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
elements.sent-2411-170: In this section, we introduce the details and considerations in the design of the XR Codec Adaptation MARL algorithms, including the Markov Decision Process (MDP) components and architectural changes to VFF-based algorithms.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Decomposed VDN } \label{dvdn_algo} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Let $\mathbf{Q}_C = \{Q_1, ... , Q_c\}$ and $\mathbf{Q}_{tot} = \{Q_{tot,1}, ... , Q_{tot,c}\}$ be a vector comprised by $Q_c$ and $Q_{tot, c}$ , representing the Q-function and the mixing networks for each $c$ decomposed reward, respectively. Initialize $\theta_c$ and $\theta'_c$ indicating the target and evaluation recurrent policies for the $c$ reward. Set the learning rate $\alpha$ and replay buffer $\mathcal{D} = \left\{ \right\}$. \STATE $\text{episode} = 0, \theta^{'}_c = \theta_c$ \FOR{environment episode $e\gets1$ to $E$} \STATE{$t = 0, o_0=\text{initial state}$} \WHILE{$done \neq True $ and $t < \text{steps/episode limit}$} \FOR{each $Q_c$ in $\mathbf{Q_C}$} \FOR{each agent $m$} \STATE $\tau^m_t = \tau^m_{t-1} \cup \{(o_t, u_{t-1})\}$ \STATE $\epsilon = \text{epsilon-schedule}(\text{step})$ \IF {$\epsilon \geq \mathcal{N}(0,1)$} \STATE{$randint(1, |U|^m); |U|^m \subset \mathbb{Z}^+$} \ENDIF \STATE {$\hat{\mathbf{Q}}^m_{c,t} \cup Q_c(\tau^m_t, u^m_t)$} \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE {$u^m_t= \begin{cases} \argmax_{u^m_{c,t}}\sum_c \hat{\mathbf{Q}}^m_{c,t}~~~~\text{with probability }1 - \epsilon randint(1, |U|^m); |U|^m \subset \mathbb{Z}^+ ~~ \text{with probability }\epsilon \end{cases}$} \STATE {$\mathbf{u}_t = \{u^1_t, ..., u^m_t\}$} \STATE Get reward $r_t$ and next state $s_{t+1}$ \STATE $\mathcal{D} = \mathcal{D} \cup \left\{(s_t, \mathbf{u}_t, r_1, ..., r_c, s_{t+1} )\right\} $ \STATE{$\text{steps}=\text{steps}+1$} \ENDWHILE \IF {$|\mathcal{D}| > \text{batch-size}$} \STATE {$b \leftarrow$ random batch of episodes from $\mathcal{{D}}$} \FOR {each timestep $t$ in each episode in batch $b$} \STATE {$Q_{tot,c} = \sum_n Q_{n,c}(\tau^n_t,u_t^n)$} \ENDFOR \STATE {$\mathcal{L}_c(\theta_c)=\sum\limits_{i=1}^b\left[\left(y_i^{tot,c} - Q_{tot,c}(\boldsymbol{\tau}, \mathbf{u}, s; \theta_c) \right)^2\right]$} \STATE {$\Delta \theta_c = \nabla\theta_c(\mathcal{L}_c(\theta_c))^2$} \STATE {$\theta_c = \theta_c - \alpha \Delta \theta_c$} \ENDIF \IF {hard update steps have passed} \STATE {$\theta^{'}_c = \theta_c$} \ENDIF \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Decomposed QMIX } \label{dqmix_algo} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Let $\mathbf{Q}_C = \{Q_1, ... , Q_c\}$ and $\mathbf{Q}_{tot,C} = \{Q_{tot,1}, ... , Q_{tot,c}\}$ be a vector comprised by $Q_c$ and $Q_{tot, c}$ , representing the Q-function and the mixing networks for each $c$ decomposed reward, respectively. Initialize $\theta_c$ and $\theta'_c$ indicating the target and evaluation recurrent policies for the $c$ reward, the parameters of mixing network, agent networks, and hypernetwork. Set the learning rate $\alpha$ and replay buffer $\mathcal{D} = \left\{ \right\}$. \STATE $\text{episode} = 0, \theta^{'}_c = \theta_c$ \FOR{environment episode $e\gets1$ to $E$} \STATE{$t = 0, o_0=\text{initial state}$} \WHILE{$done \neq True $ and $t < \text{steps/episode limit}$} \FOR{each $Q_c$ in $\mathbf{Q_C}$} \FOR{each agent $m$} \STATE $\tau^m_t = \tau^m_{t-1} \cup \{(o_t, u_{t-1})\}$ \STATE $\epsilon = \text{epsilon-schedule}(\text{step})$ \IF {$\epsilon \geq \mathcal{N}(0,1)$} \STATE{$randint(1, |U|^m); |U|^m \subset \mathbb{Z}^+$} \ENDIF \STATE {$\hat{\mathbf{Q}}^m_{c,t} \cup Q_c(\tau^m_t, u^m_t)$} \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE {$u^m_t= \begin{cases} \argmax_{u^m_{c,t}}\sum_c \hat{\mathbf{Q}}^m_{c,t}~~~~\text{with probability }1 - \epsilon randint(1, |U|^m); |U|^m \subset \mathbb{Z}^+ ~~ \text{with probability }\epsilon \end{cases}$} \STATE {$\mathbf{u}_t = \{u^1_t, ..., u^m_t\}$} \STATE Get reward $r_t$ and next state $s_{t+1}$ \STATE $\mathcal{D} = \mathcal{D} \cup \left\{(s_t, \mathbf{u}_t, r_1, ..., r_c, s_{t+1} )\right\} $ \STATE{$\text{steps}=\text{steps}+1$} \ENDWHILE \IF {$|\mathcal{D}| > \text{batch-size}$} \STATE {$b \leftarrow$ random batch of episodes from $\mathcal{{D}}$} \FOR {each timestep $t$ in each episode in batch $b$} \STATE {$Q_{tot,c} = Mixing-network~(Q_{1,c}(\tau^1_t,u_t^1),\dots,Q_{n,c}(\tau^n_t,u_t^n)$;} \STATE {Hypernetwork$(s_t; \theta_c))$} \STATE {Calculate target $Q_{tot,c}$ using $Mixing-network$ with $Hypernetwork(s_t; \theta^{'}_c))$} \ENDFOR \STATE {$\mathcal{L}_c(\theta_c)=\sum\limits_{i=1}^b\left[\left(y_i^{tot,c} - Q_{tot,c}(\boldsymbol{\tau}, \mathbf{u}, s; \theta_c) \right)^2\right]$} \STATE {$\Delta \theta_c = \nabla\theta_c(\mathcal{L}_c(\theta_c))^2$} \STATE {$\theta_c = \theta_c - \alpha \Delta \theta_c$} \ENDIF \IF {hard update steps have passed} \STATE {$\theta^{'}_c = \theta_c$} \ENDIF \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-191: Each reward component $c$ is shaped as follows: \begin{equation}R_{c,t}^{XR} = \hat{r}_{c,t}^{XR} + F_c(x_{c,t}^{XR},\hat{x}_{c,t}^{XR}),\end{equation} where $F_c$ is the potential function of the $c\in C$ reward component, $x \in \{d, p, th\}$ describing each measured KPI used in the calculation of the rewards (delay, PDR, DRR) and $x_{t ^{XR}$, $\hat{x _{t ^{XR}$ correspond to the observed and the next observed KPI of $x$ type, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Additionally, $F_c$ can be described as, \begin{equation}F_c = \gamma_p \phi(\hat{x}_{c,t}^{XR},\mathbb{g}(\hat{x}_{c,t}^{XR})) - \phi(x_{c,t}^{XR}, \mathbb{g}(x_{c,t}^{XR})),\end{equation} where $\mathbb{g}$ is a function that provides the goal given a measured KPI and $\gamma_p$ a discount factor.
elements.sent-2411-193: Notice that the goal in this case corresponds to the upper bound of each reward-defined interval.
elements.sent-2411-194: Finally, the $\phi$ function is defined as, \begin{equation}\label{phi_def} \phi(x, y, z ) = 1 - \frac{|y - x|}{z_{(norm)}}\end{equation} where $z$ is used as a normalizing factor.

elements.sent-2411-228: In this subsection, we delve into the details of the Decomposed Transformation of Q-Values or DQTRAN framework.
elements.sent-2411-229: Vanilla QTRAN presents three main blocks comprised of neural networks: (i) an individual action-value network $Q_n$, (ii) a joint action-value network $Q_{tot}$, and (iii) a state-value network $V_{tot}$.

elements.sent-2411-232: Thus, in reward decomposition, the loss function of DQTRAN takes the form of, \begin{equation}L(s, u, r, s'; \theta ) = \frac{1}{C}\left(\sum_{c \in C}L_{td}^c + \lambda_{opt}\sum_{c \in C}L_{opt}^c + \lambda_{nopt}\sum_{c \in C}L_{nopt}^c\right)\end{equation} where $\lambda_{opt}$ and $\lambda_{nopt}$ are the weighting factors for $\mathcal{L _{opt ^c$ and $\mathcal{L _{nopt ^c$\footnote{Additional details of $\mathcal{L _{opt}$ and $\mathcal{L _{nopt}$ can be found in \cite{son2019qtran , losses involved in the factorization of the joint policy, respectively.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-233: Fig. \ref{dqtran_architecture_mr presents the changes introduced to reduce the overhead in vanilla DQTRAN.}
elements.sent-2411-234: In the same fashion as DVDN and DQMIX, the changes correspond to multi-headed structures with shared layers.
elements.sent-2411-235: Multi-Headed DQTRAN (MH-DQTRAN) consists of three multi-head function approximators, where each estimator has $C$ heads: (i) each agent n's action-value network for $Q_{n ^c$, (ii) a joint action-value network for $Q_{n,tot ^c$, and (iii) a state-value network for $ V_n^c$, i.e,.}}
elements.sent-2411-236: In \cite{juozapaitis2019explainable , a metric called RDX is introduced as described in subsection \ref{explainable_metrics .}}
elements.sent-2411-237: However, such a metric can only be applied when actions are well-known.
elements.sent-2411-238: Typically, in wireless networks, actions consist of adjusting a value that can be continuous or whose dimension can become large.
elements.sent-2411-239: To overcome this situation, we propose a metric called Reward Difference Fluctuation Explanations (RDFX).
elements.sent-2411-240: It is defined as the difference between the chosen action or the maximum value of the $\bm{Q _c$ vector and the positive or negative fluctuation of the rest of the actions in a given state.}
elements.sent-2411-241: Formally, we can define it as: \begin{equation}\Delta_c(s, \bm{Q}_c, ``fluct") = max(\bm{Q}_c) - Q_{c, fluct},\end{equation} where $``fluct"$ can have two meanings, a positive fluctuation $``inc"$ or negative fluctuation $``dec"$ and $Q_{c, fluct}$ corresponds to the expected value due to increasing or decreasing value of the actions according to $``fluct"$.
elements.sent-2411-242: The positive fluctuation set can be obtained as follows.
elements.sent-2411-243: The first element of $\bm{Q _{c,inc}$ starts at the $i^{th + 1$ element of $\bm{Q _c$ where $q_i = \text{argmax (\bm{Q _c)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-244: Then, $\bm{Q _{c,inc = \{q_{i+1 , q_{i+2 , ..., q_N\}$, where $q_N$ is the final element of $\bm{Q _c$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-245: Similarly as $\bm{Q _{c,inc}$, a negative fluctuation is defined as $\bm{Q _{c,dec = \bm{Q _{c \setminus \bm{Q _{c,inc}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-247: Finally, $Q_{c, fluct}$ can be defined as, \begin{equation}\label{Q_fluctuation} Q_{c, fluct} := \begin{cases} \mathbb{E}[\bm{Q}_{c,inc}] \text{ as } ``inc" \text{if } \bm{Q}_{c,inc} \neq \varnothing \text{ else } \bm{Q}_c[N] \mathbb{E}[\bm{Q}_{c,dec}] \text{ as } ``dec" \text{if } \bm{Q}_{c,dec} \neq \varnothing \text{ else } \bm{Q}_c[0] \end{cases} \\\end{equation} Given the previous adjustment of the RDX, it is possible to utilize $\text{MSX ^+$ and $\text{MSX ^-$ utilizing RDFX instead.}}
elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation}\label{Q_fluctuation} Q_{c, fluct} :=  E[Q_{c,inc}]  as  ``inc" if  Q_{c,inc} \neq \varnothing  else  Q_c[N] E[Q_{c,dec}]  as  ``dec" if  Q_{c,dec} \neq \varnothing  else  Q_c[0] \end{equation}
elements.subsection-2411-015: Adaptive Explainable MARL via episodic gradient importance
elements.sent-2411-248: In this work, we propose reducing the complexity of decomposed XMARL algorithms, drawing inspiration from multi-task learning.
elements.sent-2411-249: As mentioned previously, we modify the internal structures of each MARL algorithm to enable the outputting of independent Q-values per $c\in C$ reward.
elements.sent-2411-250: Reward decomposition is a technique that permits the analysis of reward components post-hoc, meaning that it's not possible to utilize observed reward behavior during training.
elements.sent-2411-251: In this subsection, we aim to propose a novel online algorithm that leverages reward decomposition to extract and utilize knowledge during training.
elements.sent-2411-252: The proposed algorithm draws inspiration from GradNorm \cite{chen2018gradnorm , with a notable difference: while GradNorm normalizes training rates among tasks, our algorithm incentivizes differentiation among them.}
elements.sent-2411-253: In our context, tasks are equivalent to decomposed Q-functions.
elements.sent-2411-254: Let's begin by describing some preliminaries, to propose our solution later.
elements.sent-2411-255: Fig. \ref{importance_gradient}$\bm{(a)}$ shows how gradients behave for each reward component Q-function output.
elements.sent-2411-256: The loss is calculated in the same fashion we introduced in \eqref{total_ltd_loss .}
elements.sent-2411-257: It can be seen that the gradient dimensions differ due to the data stored concerning each decomposed reward.
elements.sent-2411-258: To remove these inherent differences driven by the acquisition of observations, actions, and rewards into the experience replay, a new loss function is proposed to scale gradients to adjust the rate at which each decomposed Q-function must be trained, \begin{equation}\label{loss_gradnorm} \mathcal{L}_e=\frac{1}{C}\sum_{c \in C} w_{c} \mathcal{L}^{c}_{\iota}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{L _e$ corresponds to the loss in episode $e$\footnote{Note that we considered the training to be performed in each episode.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: The previous weights adjust the training rate of each reward component during the training process.
elements.sent-2411-260: Now that the gradients are balanced, we utilize reward decomposition to enhance learning by assessing the significance of each reward component and incorporating it into the loss calculation.
elements.sent-2411-261: The new loss function is formulated as follows, \begin{equation}\label{loss_gradnorm1} \mathcal{L}_e=\frac{1}{C}\sum_{c \in C} w_{c} l_{c,e} \mathcal{L}^{c}_{\iota},\end{equation} where $l_{c,e}$ are the reward importance weights for each decomposed reward head.
elements.sent-2411-262: To obtain $l_{c,e}$, during each episode $e$, we collect a sequence of rewards per $c \in C$ reward type.
elements.sent-2411-263: To illustrate the statement, let's say we have three types of rewards, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{r}_{1,e} = \{r_{1,e}^1, ..., r_{1,e}^{ts}\}, \mathbf{r}_{2,e} = \{ r_{2,e}^1, ..., r_{2,e}^{ts}\}, \vdots \mathbf{r}_{c,e} = \{ r_{c,e}^1, ..., r_{c,e}^{ts}\}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $ts$ corresponds to the total of steps per episode.
elements.sent-2411-264: Next, we compute the means and variance of $r_1,...,r_c$ in episode $e$ using, \begin{equation}\bar{r}_{c,e} = \frac{1}{|\mathbf{r}_{c,e}|}\sum_{t=1}^{ts} \mathbf{r}_{c,e}^t, \label{average_rew_episode}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\sigma_{c,e} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{ts - 1} \sum_{t=1}^{ts} (\mathbf{r}_{c,e}^t - \bar{r}_{c,e} )^2}, \label{deviation_rew_episode}\end{equation} where $\bar{r _{c,e}$ and $\sigma_{c,e}$ correspond to the mean and variance of the $c$ rewards, Finally, we define the reward weight $l_{c,e}$ by utilizing softmax such as $\sum_{c\in C l_{c,e = 1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-265: The proposed equation is, \begin{equation}l_{c,e} = \frac{\text{exp}\left(\bar{r}_{c,e} + \sinh\left(\sigma_{c,e}\right) \right)}{\sum_{c\in C} \text{exp}\left(\bar{r}_{c,e} + \sinh\left(\sigma_{c,e}\right) \right).} \label{weight_reward_episode}\end{equation} The selection of the previous equation solves an undesirable behavior observed in cases where the variance term is given no importance such as in, \begin{equation}\label{no_variance_weights} l_{c,e} = \frac{\text{exp}\left(\bar{r}_{c,e} \right)}{\sum_{c\in C} \text{exp}\left(\bar{r}_{c,e} \right)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-266: Each algorithm incorporating this improvement is prefixed with "Multi-Headed Adaptive" (MHA).

elements.subsection-2411-016: Baselines: Adjust Packet Size Algorithm (APS) and proposed decomposed algorithms
elements.sent-2411-268: In this study, we compare our proposed schemes with an analog XR loopback threshold-based algorithm named Adjust Packet Size (APS) delineated in \cite{Bojovic2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-269: In the previous work, three algorithms are presented, making APS the best algorithm in terms of performance.
elements.sent-2411-270: Furthermore, we introduce variants of the algorithms VDN, QMIX, and QTRAN.
elements.sent-2411-271: Each of these variants is accompanied by a prefix indicating the type of decomposed modification it represents.. For instance,.
elements.sent-2411-272: Decomposed Vanilla: Indicated by the letter D and described by figures \ref{DVDN_DQMIX}$\bm{(1)}$ and \ref{dvdn_dqmix_mr}$\bm{(1)}$.
elements.sent-2411-273: Decomposed Multi-Headed: Indicated by the letters MH-D and described by figures \ref{DVDN_DQMIX}$\bm{(2)}$, \ref{dvdn_dqmix_mr}$\bm{(2)}$, and \ref{dqtran_architecture_mr .}
elements.sent-2411-274: Decomposed Multi-Headed Adaptive: Indicated by the letters MHA-D and described by subsection \ref{adaptive_back .}
elements.sent-2411-275: In the next section, we depict the results of the current work concerning explainability and network performance.
elements.sent-2411-276: In this section, we present the results of employing reward decomposition in MARL.
elements.sent-2411-277: We showcase the average Q-values, RDFX, and MSX metrics to facilitate a deeper understanding of the contribution of each reward component during action selection.
elements.sent-2411-278: These metrics enable enhancements for the proposed decomposed MARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-279: Additionally, we offer comprehensive comparative results among all the proposed algorithms in terms of network KPIs.
elements.sent-2411-280: We conduct simulations utilizing the ns-3 New Radio (NR) module, version 2.3 released in April 2023.
elements.sent-2411-281: Formerly known as NR-Lena, this module is integrated into the ns-3 simulator, offering simulations for 3GPP NR non-standalone cellular networks.
elements.sent-2411-282: Furthermore, we employ the ns-3 gym \cite{ns3gym module as an interface between ns-3 and Python-based agents.}
elements.sent-2411-283: RL parameters and simulation settings are delineated in Tables \ref{learning_settings and \ref{net_settings , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-284: To investigate the influence of distance on both the proposed algorithms and the existing baseline (APS), users are dispersed randomly across three distinct rings with radii ranging from 100 to 400 meters.
elements.sent-2411-285: Within each ring, users are randomly positioned within the inner radius and maintain random mobility throughout the simulation, moving at a speed of $3$ m/s.
elements.sent-2411-286: The capacity of the transmission RLC buffer is capped at 60,000 bytes.
elements.sent-2411-287: The simulation adopts an Urban Macro (UMa) channel model, with all UEs in non-line-of-sight conditions.
elements.sent-2411-288: It employs a single bandwidth part with a central frequency of 4 GHz and a bandwidth of 20 MHz.
elements.sent-2411-289: In the next subsection, we will discuss the details of the proposed evaluation framework.

elements.sent-2411-290: In this subsection, we propose an evaluation framework (Fig. \ref{explanaibility_framework ) to facilitate the analysis of our proposed decomposed MARL algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-291: As illustrated in the figure, a reference algorithm (RA) - typically the baseline - and the decomposed MARL algorithm are initialized.
elements.sent-2411-292: This initialization involves loading the network parameters (weights and biases) of all algorithms to be evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-293: Subsequently, the RA interacts with the environment, and the observations generated based on the actions taken by the RA serve as input for the decomposed algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-294: At each time step, the Q-values from each algorithm are utilized to calculate the RDFX and MSX metrics.
elements.sent-2411-295: This process occurs in the Explain block (blue colored box in Fig. \ref{explanaibility_framework ), where the Q-values of each decomposed reward are analyzed using the previously proposed metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-296: This process allows concurrent explanations of each algorithm's behavior and enables a fair comparison.
elements.subsection-2411-019: Explanation insights for reward decomposition in XR codec adaptation
elements.sent-2411-297: In this subsection, we offer a quantitative evaluation of explainable metrics derived from employing the proposed explainable framework and the trained decomposed MARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-298: The primary objective is to illustrate how wireless-related metrics, frequently utilized in reward design, can influence the formulation of RL problems.
elements.sent-2411-299: Specifically, we utilize the average Q-value, RDFX, and MSX to draw conclusions regarding the components of a predefined composite function.
elements.sent-2411-300: Finally, we present the performance of the proposed decomposed algorithms and the baseline APS in terms of network KPIs.

elements.sent-2411-311: According to the definition of RDFX, this signifies the superiority of the selected action compared to choosing a higher or lower value for the XR codec.

elements.sent-2411-315: In Fig. \ref{msx_+_dec , we illustrate the ($MSX^+$) for the selected codec parameter over selecting a lower configuration value.}
elements.sent-2411-316: This figure demonstrates a similar trend as Fig. \ref{msx_+_inc , albeit with the distinction that a lower configuration parameter assigns greater importance to the DRR.}
elements.sent-2411-317: In contrast, a higher codec configuration prioritizes delay.
elements.sent-2411-318: Intuitively, this result is expected, as reducing the codec parameter allows more flexibility to increase the data rate, whereas selecting a higher parameter emphasizes the delay component.
elements.sent-2411-319: The graph of the average negative minimal sufficient explanations $(MSX^-)$ is not included in this work, due to $(MSX^-) = \{\}$, which means that all of the agents believe that the action they have selected dominates over any fluctuation of the codec parameters.
elements.sent-2411-320: Note that, these results are solely related to this case study, but the method and framework proposed in this work can be employed in any other MARL algorithm.

elements.sent-2411-321: In this subsection, we present the results that led us to the design of the importance reward metric utilized as weights in the adaptive decomposed algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-322: The weights computed per episode tend to have a preference for the best reward in terms of magnitude, as observed in Fig. \ref{no_variance_weights_graph .}
elements.sent-2411-323: In such a figure, we present as an example the behavior of the weights during a training session of MHA-DVDN\footnote{All adaptive algorithms (MHA-DQMIX and MHA-DQTRAN) showed a similar behavior.. at $300$ m.}
elements.sent-2411-324: As observed, $w2$ corresponding to the reward component PDR is always given maximum importance with respect to the rest of the weights $w1$ and $w3$, delay, and DRR, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-325: This is undesirable, leaving no room for other components to contribute to the learning process and making training unstable.
elements.sent-2411-326: To solve this issue we include the variance term and use $f(x) = \sinh{x ; x > 0$ to add importance to those reward components with high variance throughout training.}
elements.sent-2411-327: In Fig. \ref{variance_weights_graph , it can be seen how such modification helps to give more importance to low mean value rewards.}

elements.subsection-2411-021: Performance of the proposed decomposed MARL algorithms
elements.sent-2411-328: In this subsection, we demonstrate that reward decomposition not only provides valuable insights into the design of MARL algorithms but also shows that the decomposed architecture can be utilized to enhance network performance while offering additional information.
elements.sent-2411-329: In Fig. \ref{APS_DVDN_DQMIX_DQTRAN , we present comparative graphs illustrating performance in terms of network KPIs: XR index, jitter, delay, and PLR, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-330: Each subplot showcases the performance achieved by distance and the proposed VFF decomposed algorithms alongside the baseline APS.
elements.sent-2411-331: It can be observed that when UEs are closer to their respective base station ($d \in \{200,300\}$ m), the performance is similar, except in terms of PLR.
elements.sent-2411-332: However, the difference becomes significant as the distance increases to $400$ m, corresponding to scenarios where UEs are more impacted by propagation path loss.
elements.sent-2411-333: Additionally, we provide the average percentage gains over the APS algorithm in Table \ref{compartative_table .}
elements.sent-2411-334: Indicated by a green arrow up $\color{green (\uparrow)$ and a blue arrow down $(\color{blue}\downarrow)$, we denote the percentage increase or decrease of the network KPIs.}
elements.sent-2411-335: For example, we observe that in most cases, the variants corresponding to QMIX perform the best (indicated by bold font).
elements.sent-2411-336: Furthermore, architectural changes inspired by multi-task learning contribute positively to KPI improvements across almost all algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-337: The best algorithm, MHA-DQMIX, offers an average improvement of up to $10.7 \%$, $41.4 \%$, $33.3 \%$, and $67.9\%$ in terms of XR index, jitter, delay, and PLR, respectively.
elements.subsection-2411-022: Potential applications in the telecommunications industry
elements.sent-2411-338: XRL-based reward decomposition techniques have great potential for telecommunications companies.
elements.sent-2411-339: Below, we list some potential future applications and direct applications in the industry:.
elements.sent-2411-340: RL algorithm selection: Given $M$ RL algorithms, a 6G/ML engineer can observe how reward design in the Markov Decision Process (MDP) affects each algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-341: This observation can lead to an informed selection of the best algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-342: Post-hoc RL algorithm failure analysis: At a failure event, reward decomposition allows post-hoc analysis of the performance of RL algorithms by inspecting the decomposed rewards in each observed state.
elements.sent-2411-343: Note that a logging system must be operating to enable such analysis.
elements.sent-2411-344: Reward design in RL algorithms: Reward decomposition allows the optimization of RL algorithms by analyzing the importance of each component of the composite reward objective function.
elements.sent-2411-345: This can potentially lead to a reduction in the reward composition size and, consequently, a decrease in the number of Application Programming Interface (API) requests to the cloud, vendor, or analytics provider to obtain any KPIs utilized to derive each reward.

elements.sent-2411-346: In this paper, we propose Value Function Factorization (VFF)-based Explainable Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (XMARL) algorithms that utilize reward decomposition in XR codec adaptation problems.
elements.sent-2411-347: We present theoretical bounds that VFF-XMARL algorithms can be decomposed and we introduce architectural changes to several VFF-XMARL algorithms, such as Value Decomposition Networks (VDN), QMIX (Mixture of Q-Values), and QTRAN (Q-Value Factorization).
elements.sent-2411-348: Additionally, we propose utilizing ideas from multi-task learning to reduce the overhead of vanilla decomposed MARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-349: Moreover, we introduce a novel metric called Reward Difference Fluctuation Explanations (RDFX), which is suitable when actions correspond to configuration metrics typically found in wireless network problems.
elements.sent-2411-350: Furthermore, we present a solution that allows providing explanations in an online manner and using them to improve current algorithms, named Multi-Headed Adaptive.
elements.sent-2411-351: This proposal utilizes network gradients and leverages reward decomposition to enhance the action selection of the XMARL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-352: We observe that for XR codec adaptation, the most crucial reward component corresponds to the packet delivery ratio (PDR), according to the minimal sufficient explanation (MSX) metric.
elements.sent-2411-353: In conclusion, the utilization of MARL algorithms using reward decomposition in wireless networks not only provides a better understanding of the design of composite rewards but also can improve trust and network performance if careful design is performed.
